coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of nanoporpous graphitic carbon nitride photocatalyst coupled with N-doped graphene quantum dots for efficient photo-degradation dyes under nature solar light radiation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Congxu
Zhou, Yaya
Sun, Youyi
Guo, Guizheng
Fu, Qian
Xiong, Zhiyuan
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: Nanoporous graphitic carbon nitride modified with N-doped graphene quantums dots (N-GQD@npg-C3N4) was prepared via a one-pot co-sintering method. The presented N-GQD@npg-C3N4 showed a high photocatalytic activity (PCA) for the degradation of organic dyes under solar radiation. This result can be ascribed to two reasons: (i) the uniform distribution of N-GQDs on the surface of npg-C3N4, which allows for an accelerated electron transfer from npg-C3N4 to N-GQD; and (ii) the unique nanoporous structure of npg-C3N4, providing a large active surface area. This work opens up a new avenue for the utilization of a robust, cost-effective and metal-free photocatalyst in practical applications, e.g. waste water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-free photocatalyst
In-situ synthesis
Nanoporous
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure N-GQD, npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of N-GQD. The inset is the optical photograph of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of N-GQD (a)0, (b)0.3 wt%, (c)0.6 wt%, (d)0.9 wt%, (e)1.2 wt% and (f)1.5 wt%.
FT-IR spectra of (A) npg-C3N4, (G) N-GQDs and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of (B)0.3 wt%, (C)0.6 wt%, (D)0.9 wt%, (E)1.2 wt% and (F)1.5 wt%.
(A) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (B) the corresponding BJH pore size distribution of (a)bulk-C3N4, (b)npg-C3N4 and (c)sample 3.
SEM images of (A) bulk g-C3N4 and (B) sample 3; (C) TEM images of the sample 3.

(A)UV-DRS and (B) Band gap energies of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of (a)0, (b)0.3 wt%, (c)0.6 wt%, (d)0.9 wt%, (e)1.2 wt% and (f)1.5 wt%;(C) Proposed mechanism of the formation sub-band after combination npg-C3N4 with N-GQDs.

(A) Transient photocurrent curves of (a) N-GQDs and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of (b)0, (c)0.3 wt%, (d)0.6 wt%, (e)0.9 wt%, (f)1.2 wt% and (g)1.5 wt%; (B) The upconversion PL spectra of N-GQDs irradiated under 590–750.0 nm light;(C) PL spectrum of npg-C3N4 and Sample 3.

(A) UV–vis spectra of RhB solution (a) without and (b) with nature solar irradiation (60.0 min) in the absence of the photocatalyst; (B) Photodegration of RhB with different samples. (a) bulk g-C3N4, N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of (b)0, (c)0.3 wt%, (d)0.6 wt%, (e)0.9 wt%, (f)1.2 wt% and (g)1.5 wt%; (C) UV–vis spectra of MB and (D) MO solution with various photocatalytic degradation time. The inset of (A) is optical photograph of the photocatalytic degradation experiment of RhB solution.
Photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalyst as function of (A) pH, (B) initial RhB concentration and (C) photocatalyst amount.

(A) Stability study for the photocatalytic dyes degradation by N-GQD@npg-C3N4; (B) XRD patterns of before and after three consecutive photocatalytic reactions; SEM images of sample 3 (C) before and (D) after three consecutive photocatalytic reactions.
Effects of different scavengers on the photocatalytic activity of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 under solar light irradiation.
(A) HPLC spectra of 4-nitrophenol and the intermediate products during the photocatalytic process and (B) the proposed degradation pathway.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of N-GQD@npg-C3N4.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for degradation dyes over N-GQD@npg-C3N4 under solar light irradiation.
The N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of N-GQDs.

row.entry: The volume of N-GODs aqueous solution (mL)

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of nanoporpous graphitic carbon nitride photocatalyst coupled with N-doped graphene quantum dots for efficient photo-degradation dyes under nature solar light radiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y. Sun, Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoporous graphitic carbon nitride modified with N-doped graphene quantums dots (N-GQD@npg-C3N4) was prepared via a one-pot co-sintering method. The presented N-GQD@npg-C3N4 showed a high photocatalytic activity (PCA) for the degradation of organic dyes under solar radiation. This result can be ascribed to two reasons: (i) the uniform distribution of N-GQDs on the surface of npg-C3N4, which allows for an accelerated electron transfer from npg-C3N4 to N-GQD; and (ii) the unique nanoporous structure of npg-C3N4, providing a large active surface area. This work opens up a new avenue for the utilization of a robust, cost-effective and metal-free photocatalyst in practical applications, e.g. waste water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution due to dyeing industry has created a huge environmental problem, which causes serious health-risk to human and threats the survival of many animals. Since the synthetic dyes in waste water are chemically stable, a wide range of methods has been developed to remove these dyes, including physical adsorption, bio-degradation, chemical precipitation and photocatalytic degradation [1,2]. Photocatalytic degradation is considered a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach for the mitigation of dye pollution [1,3–5]. Over the past few decades, various nanostructured semiconductors have been employed as photocatalysts for the degradation of synthetic dyes under visible light [3–5]. Among various semiconductors, the graphitic carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) with two-dimensional (2D) topology have received splendid attention because of their semiconductor properties, suitable electronic structure (Eg = 2.7 eV), environmentally compatible composition, excellent physicochemical stability and “earth-abundant” nature [6–9]. However, the fast recombination of photo-generated charge carriers results in a low photocatalytic efficiency, which in turn hampers the practical application of g-C3N4 [1,10–12]. In order to overcome this obstruction, a series of methods have been developed, such as elements doping, surface hetero-junction design with other materials, noble metal deposition, and texturization [13–17]. Although the introduction of noble metals has been demonstrated to be a facile and efficient way to enhance photocatalytic performance [16,17], this method is limited in large-scale application due to the use of rare and expensive noble metals. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensive applied in photocatalytic field due to their excellent electron transfer, electron reservoir [18,19] and upconversion luminescence properties [20]. For example, Jiang et al. reported the synthesis of GQDs decorated ZnS for the efficient degradation of methylene orange [21]. Yan et al. developed a GQDs decorated g-C3N4 for the degradation of antibiotics under visible light [22]. Zou et al. reported the use of similar catalytic material for photocatalytic H2 evolution [23]. On the other hand, the use of nanoporous graphitic carbon nitrides (npg-C3N4) could efficiently promote charge separation and increase surface area, and hence greatly enhance photocatalytic performance [1,24]. We thus saw this as an opportunity to develop a novel photocatalytic system, which harnesses the synergetic effect between GQDs and npg-C3N4 for the efficient degradation of organic dye pollutants. Furthermore, photocatalytic degradation dyes was under simulated sunlight [25–27].

In this work, the precursors N-GQDs and npg-C3N4 were separately synthesized and the photocatalyst N-GQDs@npg-C3N4 was then prepared via a one-pot co-sintering method. The resultant N-GQDs@npg-C3N4 was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM etc. and its photocatalytic activity was also investigated in detail for the degradation of organic days under nature sunlight. This study thus represents a novel design for the development of highly efficient, metal-free photocatalysts for environmental applications, such as removing organic dyes from water.

N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was prepared by an in-situ method as shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, N-GQDs were synthesized according to the previous literature [21]. Typically, 2.5 mmoL citric acid and 10.0 mmol urea were dissolved in 12.0 mL deonized water under stirring. The solution was transferred into a 50.0 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160.0 °C for 8 h. The final products were collected via centrifugation (8000 rpm) and re-dispersed in 40.0 mL deonized water.

Secondly, npg-C3N4 was prepared as follow: 20.0 g melamine and 5.0 g Triton X-100 were dispersed in 200.0 mL deionized water under stirring for 1.0 h, and then the mixture was heated at 100 °C under stirring for 1.0 h. 4.0 mL concentrated sulfuric acid (98.0 wt%) was added, and the mixture was continued to react for another 1.0 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the precipitate was collected via centrifugation, washed with ethanol and distilled water several times to remove excess Triton X-100, and dried at 80.0 °C for 12.0 h. The obtained sample was dispersed in 5.0 mL N-GQDs solution (in deonized water), followed by evaporation in the oven at 60.0 °C. Thereafter, the mixture was calcined at 500.0 °C and 580.0 °C for 2.0 h at a heating rate of 2.0 °C·min−1, respectively. The resultant products were collected and milled into a fine powder. Notably, the photocatalysts N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various N-GQDs contents were synthesized by controlling the volume of N-GQDs solution as shown in Table 1 . For a comparison, the npg-C3N4 and bulk g-C3N4 were also synthesized according to previously reported processes.
X-ray diffraction (HAO YUAN) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray powder diffraction with Cu-Kα (λ = 0.1546 nm) radiation.

The morphology and size of the obtained samples were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360).
UV absorption spectra were recorded on a ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (cary 5000).
The fluorescence of N-GQDs was recorded on a fluorospectro photometer(DF 2000).
The specific surface areas were measured using the BET method by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77.0 K using an automatic surface are and size analyzer (3H-2000PS2).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained on a cary 5000 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as a reference.

Electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI-660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China), with a conventional three-electrode system where a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter), a standard calomel electrode and a graphite electrode were used as working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrodes were prepared by dip-coating: 5.0 μL suspension of photocatalyst (2 mg mL−1, in anhydrous ethanol) was dropped onto the glassy carbon electron followed by coating with 5.0 μL of nafion solution (0.5 wt%). The electrolyte (0.5 M Na2SO4) was purged with nitrogen.
As for photocurrent measurements, a 300 W Xe lamp was used as the source of simulated solar irradiation.

The concentrations of dye and by-products of the photocatalytic process were analyzed with a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Ultimate 3000) equipped with a chromatographic separator column (C18, 250.0 mm, 4.6 mm, 5.0 μm) which is maintained at 20.0 °C. Mobile phase consists of phase A (0.01 mol/L biphosphate-phosphoric acid buffer solution, pH = 3) and phase B (methanol) with the same flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. Automatic injection sample volume is 20.0 μL.

The photocatalytic activities of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 were evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of dyes under nature sunlight (GPS coordination: North University of China, N37.998, E112.4726). Typically, photocatalysts was poured in the RhB aqueous solution and magnetically stirred in the dark for 1.0 h to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium before the mixture was irradiated under sunlight. At certain time intervals, a 4.0 mL suspension was taken out and the photocatalysts were removed via centrifugation. Then, the supernatant was characterized using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 553.0 nm.

The presence of active radical species and their role on photocatalysis were tested by a radical trapping experiment using sacrificial agents. Specifically, ammonium oxalate (AO), tert-butanol(t-BA) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as hole (h+), hydroxyl radical scavenger (OH) and superoxide radical (O2
–) scavenger, respectively. 1.0 mM of these scavengers were added into the photocatalyst-RhB solution (50.0 mg, 16.0 mg/L) in 100.0 mL beaker. The solution containing RhB, photocatalyst and scavenger was then exposed to visible light for different irradiation time. At least three tests were conducted for each scavenger (tert-BA, AO and BQ).

The successfully preparation of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was firstly confirmed by the XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 1 , the N-GQDs showed a broad and strong peak at around 23.5° and the evident diffraction peaks at 27.3° and 13.1° corresponding to (002) and (100) planes of npg-C3N4 were also observed [27,28]. In addition, all the obtained N-GQD@npg-C3N4 samples displayed similar patterns with pure npg-C3N4. Meanwhile, the peak of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 at 27.3° decreased with increasing the content of N-GQDs. These results demonstrated the successful incorporation of N-GQDs on the surface of npg-C3N4 [10,20,29], which would slightly affected the phase structure of npg-C3N4 [22]. Furthermore, digital photographs of these samples were showed in Fig. 1 as inset. The pure npg-C3N4 showed a dark yellow color, consisting with previous report [22]. In contrast, the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 samples exhibited light yellow to gray color due to be introduction of N-GQD, suggesting the change in energy structure of g-C3N4 [22].

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of the npg-C3N4, N-GQDs and N-GQD@npg-C3N4. The broad peak at 3210.0 cm−1 in the spectrum of N-GQDs can be assigned to the stretching mode of OH and the vibration of NH [30]. The peak at 1626.0 cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretching mode of COOH groups [29]. The peaks at 1395.0 cm−1 and 871.0 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO functional groups and epoxied groups, respectively [31]. For pristine npg-C3N4, the peaks at 1622.0 cm−1, 1391.0 cm−1, 1310.0 cm−1 and 1217.0 cm−1 were ascribed to the typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles [32]. In addition, the characteristic peak at 890.0 cm−1 can be attributed to the s-triazine ring modes [6]. It could be concluded that all the characteristic vibrational peaks related to npg-C3N4 or N-GQDs can be observed in the spectra of N-GQD@npg-C3N4. Notably, the intensity of the peaks at 1109.0 cm−1 enhanced when increasing the content of N-GQDs. These results thus suggested the successful combination of the npg-C3N4 and N-GQDs and the high structural integrity of the resultant N-GQD@npg-C3N4.

Fig. 3A and Fig. 3B shows the typical adsorption/desorption isotherm and the pore size distribution of the bulk g-C3N4, npg-C3N4 and the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with 0.9 wt% of N-GQD (sample 3 in Table 1) at 77.0 K, respectively. They showed similar type IV isotherms shapes, indicating the formation of mesoporous structures (according to IUPAC classification) as shown in Fig. 3A [33,34]. The BET surface area of the bulk g-C3N4, npg-C3N4 and sample 3 was of 17.8 m2 g−1, 94.2 m2 g−1, 93.7 m2 g−1, respectively. The present npg-C3N4 and the nanocomposites N-GQD@npg-C3N4 clearly displayed larger surface area compared to the bulk g-C3N4, which possessed nanoporous structure. It is interesting that the npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 exhibited similar BET surface area, which may be attributed to the uniform distribution of N-GQDs on the surface of npg-C3N4. Indeed, this result has been rarely reported in previous literature [35,36]. The pore diameter of bulk g-C3N4, npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was further determined and compared in Fig. 3B. Both the npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 showed a strong peak at 5.6 nm, indicating the formation of nanoscale pores without blockage from the N-GQDs. As well-known, the larger surface area is favorable to photocatalytic activity, providing more active sites and adsorbing more dyes on the surface of the photocatalyst [37].

The morphology of the bulk g-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 (sample 3 in Table 1) was characterized by the SEM measurements. The bulk g-C3N4 presented a smooth surface and irregular block structure (Fig. 4A), agreeing with previous report [22,35]. Contrarily, the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 showed a foam-like skeleton with numerous nanoscale pores (Fig. 4B). We further characterized the formed N-GQD@npg-C3N4 by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4C, we observed a nanosheet with nanoporous structure. In addition, the N-GQDs were observed as black spots, which uniformly distributed on the surface of npg-C3N4 with an average diameter of 4.8 nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-GQD showed an interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (101) spacing of graphitic carbon [29]. These results provided solid evidence for the formation of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with nanoporous structure and uniform distribution.

To investigate the synergetic effect between N-GQDs and npg-C3N4 on the light absorption properties, the npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 were investigated by the UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) as shown in Fig. 5A. The fundamental absorption edge of the npg-C3N4 was found to be 482.0 nm, while the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 extended the ultaviolet-visible absorption over the entire wavelength range investigated. The N-GQD@npg-C3N4 also showed a stronger broad background absorption in comparison with pure npg-C3N4. When the weight fraction of N-GQDs reached 1.5 wt% (sample 5 in Table 1), the absorption in the visible light region significantly increased. The optical band gap of the as-prepared samples can be estimated from the Tauc plot (i.e., the curve of converted (ahv)2 versus hv from the UV–vis DRS, in which a, h, and v are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively) [38]. As shown in Fig. 5B the Eg value of npg-C3N4 was about 2.74 eV, consisting with previous report [16,24]. We also observed that the optical band-gap decreased gradually with increasing N-GQDs content. This may be attributed to the introduction of C, leading to the formation of a sub-band state in the band gap [39]. As shown in Fig. 5C, the npg-C3N4 and N-GQDs can be used as electron acceptor and electron donor respectively, resulting in a decrease in energy band of npg-C3N4 and hence enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency.

For photocatalytic applications, the effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes is crucial [14]. In this study, the photocurrent of all samples was characterized to investigate the electron-hole separation effect. As shown in Fig. 6A, the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 displayed the highest photocurrent value, suggesting the highest separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in comparison with the N-GQDs and npg-C3N4. The photoluminescence (PL) property of the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was carried out at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 6B). The N-GQDs exhibited good upconversion properties. Specifically, when they were excited at 590.0–750.0 nm, we observed the emission of light at a shorter wavelength of 400.0–550.0 nm. This can enhance the visible light absorption ability of the npg-C3N4. Fig. 6C showed the PL spectra of the pure npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4. For pure npg-C3N4 (excited at 360.0 nm) we observed a strong emission peak centered at about 436.0 nm, suggesting the recombination of the excited electron-hole pairs. However, in the case of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 we observed PL quenching. This result thus indicated that the N-GQDs could significantly suppress the recombination of these pairs and hence improve the separation efficiency of npg-C3N4.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the prepared N-GQD@npg-C3N4, we conducted the photocatalytic degradations of RhB using various photocatalysts (npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4) under direct solar irradiation (the inset of Fig. 7A). Firstly, in a control experiment negligible degradation of RhB was observed under nature solar irradiation in the absence of the photocatalyst (Fig. 7A). In contrast, significance degradation of RhB occurred in the presence of photocatalyst, indicating the high photocatalytic activity of npg-C3N4 and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 and an accelerated degradation of organic dyes under sunlight (Fig. 7B). This result can be ascribed to the generation of electrons and holes under solar irradiation, leading to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS, i.e. O2 − and OH−) for the degradation of RhB. In addition, both npg-C3N4 (89.4%) and N-GQD@npg-C3N4 (86.1%~97.2%) exhibited higher photocatalytic performance toward the degradation of RhB compared to bulk g-C3N4 (61.8%) under same conditions. We also found the photocatalytic activity of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 firstly increased and then slightly decreased with the increase in N-GQDs contents. Specifically, the dye removal rate of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 with various contents of 0.3 wt%, 0.6 wt%, 0.9 wt%, 1.2 wt% and 1.5 wt% was 93.3%, 95.6%, 97.2%, 89.4%, 88.7% and 86.1% under solar irradiation for 1.0 h, respectively. This result may be attributed to the decrease in active surface area of the npg-C3N4 when loading high content of N-GQDs. Encouraged by these results, we further conducted photocatalytic degradations of other organic dyes, such as methyl blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) using N-GQD@npg-C3N4 as photocatalyst (Fig. 7C and Fig. 7D). The success in these experiments clearly implied that N-GQD@npg-C3N4 can effectively remove organic dyes from water via photocatalytic degradation method.

The effects of the initial RhB concentration, pH of water and the amount of photocatalyst applied on the photocatalytic activity of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 (sample 3 in Table 1) were investigated in detail. Fig. 8A showed the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of sample 3 as a function of pH (ranging from 3.0 to 11.0) under solar irradiation. The efficiency firstly increased and then decreased with increase in pH and reached the maximum value (ca.100%) at pH 7.0. This phenomenon can be attributed to the pH-dependent adsorption of dyes and photocatalyst [35]. Fig. 8B showed that the photocatalytic efficiency of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 decreased when increasing initial RhB concentration from 6.0 to 26 mg/L. For example, when the initial RhB concentration was 26.0 mg/L, the degradation efficiency was only 26.0%. The photocatalytic efficiency sharply increased when increasing the initial concentration of photocatalyst from 0.5 g/L to 1.0 g/L, and then became consistent when further increasing initial concentration to 2.5 g/L. This result may be attributed to the low transparency of the reaction solution when applying high concentration of RhB or N-GQD@npg-C3N4.

Due to its heterogeneous nature, the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 allows for easier post-reaction separation. As shown in Fig. 9A, we conducted photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes using recovered photocatalyst and observed similar photocatalytic activity after three cycles. We further characterized the recovered N-GQD@npg-C3N4 by XRD and SEM measurements and found identical patterns and morphologies after three consecutive cycles (Fig. 9B–D). All these results provide solid evidence of the high structural stability and reusability of the N-GQD@npg-C3N4.

In order to determine the active species generated during the photo-degradation process, we carried out a series of radical trapping experiments. Tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), ammonium oxalate (AO) and benzoquinone (BQ) were employed as hydroxyl radicals (OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (O2 −) scavengers, respectively (Fig. 10 ) [35]. Under solar irradiation, the N-GQD@npg-C3N4 displayed identical photocatalytic degradation efficiency in the absence or presence of TBA and AO. In contrast, we found that the addition of BQ significantly reduced the degradation efficiency of RhB to 16.4%. These results indicated that the OH and h+ were minor active species, while the O2 − was the predominant reactive species in the photocatalytic process.

Fig. 11A shows the HPLC profiles of RhB solution as a function of irradiation time. The peak at 8.0 min was assigned to RhB and its intensity is expected to decrease with increase in reaction time. After the photocatalytic reaction, we observed three new peaks at 2.7 min, 4.4 min and 7.0 min, which were attributed to the degradation products of RhB as shown in Fig. 11B. Furthermore, all these peaks disappeared after 60 min, indicating complete degradation of RhB and the intermediate products.

Based on the results obtained from trapping experiments, PL, DRS, and photocurrent measurements, we further hypothesize the possible photocatalytic mechanism of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 as illustrated in Scheme 2 . When N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was irradiated under visible light or sunlight, the N-GQD works as an electron shuttle, which can facilitate the electron transfer from npg-C3N4 to the dissolved oxygen (O2) in water, resulting in effective electron-hole separation and the generation of O2 −. Meanwhile, the holes left on the VB of npg-C3N4 can react with water to yield hydroxyl radicals (OH). After a series of reactions, the generated radicals attack dye in the close vicinity, resulting in the degradation and mineralization of RhB.

In this work, a new metal-free photocatalyst N-GQD@npg-C3N4 was prepared via a facile one-pot co-sintering method. The prepared photocatalyst possesses unique nanoporous structure with uniform distribution of N-GQDs on the surface of npg-C3N4. Such metal-free photocatalyst displayed high photocatalytic activity, structural stability and good reusability in the photo-degradation of organic dyes under solar irradiation. This study thus demonstrates the potential application of N-GQD@npg-C3N4 in the treatment of waste water and mitigation of organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants (51773184), and the Shanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (201701D121046).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Superior nanoporous graphitic carbon nitride photocatalyst coupled with CdS quantum dots for photodegradation of RhB

g-C3N4/Nb2O5 heterostructures tailored by sonochemical synthesis: Enhanced photocatalytic performance in oxidation of emerging pollutants driven by visible radiation
Appl. Catal. B Environ.
Mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride as photo-catalyst for oxidative desulfurization with oxygen

Appl. Catal. B Environ.

Appl. Catal. B Environ.
Isotype heterostructure of bulk and nanosheets of graphitic carbon nitride for efficient visible light photodegradation of methylene blue
